title: Collaborative project — production roles
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to selecting, justifying, and executing a production role within the IB Theatre HL Collaborative Project. It focuses on aligning practical work with the assessment criteria for the process portfolio (Criteria B and C), ensuring students can document their contribution and its impact effectively to achieve top-band marks.
learningObjectives: Identify the key responsibilities of major production roles (e.g., Director, Designer, Stage Manager).
Construct a perceptive and well-reasoned justification for a chosen production role, linking it to the stimulus and group intentions (Criterion B).
Develop strategies for documenting the process, contribution, and impact of a production role for the process portfolio (Criterion C).
Critically evaluate the efficacy of one's own contribution to the collaborative process and the final performance.
Understand how to synthesise practical work with theoretical understanding and reflective analysis in portfolio entries.

simpleExplanation.title: Wearing the Right Hat: Mastering Your Production Role
simpleExplanation.summary: In the collaborative project, you create an original piece of theatre as a group. Your 'production role' is the specific job you take on, like director or designer. This isn't just a title; it's the specific lens through which you contribute your skills and ideas, and it's a major part of how your individual work is assessed.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of creating your play like a complex surgical operation. The whole team (the ensemble) needs to save the patient (create a successful performance). You can't have everyone trying to be the lead surgeon. You need specialists: an anaesthetist (Sound Designer controlling the mood), a scrub nurse (Stage Manager organising the tools and flow), and the surgeon (Director guiding the main action). Your production role is your specialism. The operation only succeeds if each specialist expertly performs their distinct task while communicating flawlessly with the rest of the team.
steps: Research & Propose: Investigate various production roles. Propose the one that best fits your skills, the project's stimulus, and the group's artistic vision. This forms the basis of your justification.

Define & Document: Clearly outline your role's responsibilities with your group. From day one, document your process: your research, ideas, plans, and communications. This is the raw material for your portfolio.

Execute & Collaborate: Fulfil your duties with precision. A lighting designer creates lighting states; a director blocks scenes. Constantly communicate your progress and decisions, showing how your work integrates with others'.

Reflect & Evaluate: In your portfolio, don't just state what you did. Analyse *why* you made certain choices, evaluate their *efficacy* (how well they worked), and explain their *impact* on the audience and the overall piece.

flashcards.back: A specific creative or logistical function undertaken by an individual student within the collaborative ensemble (e.g., Director, Set Designer, Stage Manager).
The role responsible for shaping the artistic vision, guiding the performers, and ensuring a cohesive synthesis of all theatrical elements. Focuses on blocking, pacing, and interpretation.
The role responsible for the organisational and logistical aspects of the production. Manages rehearsal schedules, creates the prompt book, and calls cues during the performance.
The role responsible for the design, installation, and operation of all lighting. Uses elements like colour, intensity, and direction to create mood, focus, and atmosphere.
The role responsible for the visual appearance of the performers. Designs and sources clothing and accessories that communicate character, status, and the world of the play.
The assessment criterion for the process portfolio that evaluates the student's ability to provide a well-reasoned and informed justification for their choice of production role.
The assessment criterion for the process portfolio that evaluates the student's documentation and evaluation of their role's contribution, its efficacy, and its impact on the final performance.
A key term in Criterion C. It means the effectiveness of your contribution. Did your choices work as intended? Why or why not?
A key term in Criterion C. It refers to the effect your contribution had on the ensemble, the process, the audience, and the final theatrical product.
A common pitfall where the responsibilities of different production roles become blurred, leading to confusion, conflict, or a lack of clear ownership over specific areas of the production.
The master copy of the script, maintained by the Stage Manager, containing all actor blocking, and lighting, sound, and set cues. It is a key piece of evidence for a Stage Manager's portfolio.
The blending of different elements (e.g., acting, design, sound) to create a coherent and unified whole. A director's primary goal and a key concept for all roles to consider.

faq.q: Can I invent a new production role that isn't on the standard list?
faq.a: Yes, you can. Roles like 'Dramaturg', 'Choreographer', or 'Projection/Media Designer' are perfectly valid, provided the role is essential to your group's specific concept. You must provide a very clear definition of its responsibilities and a robust justification (Criterion B) for why this unique role is necessary for your project.

faq.q: What if someone in my group isn't fulfilling their role properly?
faq.a: This is a common challenge in any collaborative process. First, try to resolve it as a group through open communication. In your portfolio, you can reflect on this. Document the challenge it presented to the ensemble and to your own work, and detail the steps you (and the group) took to mitigate the problem. This demonstrates your understanding of collaborative dynamics and your problem-solving skills, which is a valuable aspect of the process.

faq.q: How much of my portfolio should be about my production role versus the general group process?
faq.a: Your production role is the specific lens through which you view and document the entire project. While you must demonstrate collaboration (Criterion A), the bulk of your portfolio for Criteria B and C should be firmly rooted in your individual contribution through your chosen role. A good ratio to aim for is 80% focus on your role's process and impact, and 20% on the wider collaborative context and how your work fits within it.

faq.q: Can two people share a role, for example, co-directors?
faq.a: This is strongly discouraged by the IB. The assessment is individual, and sharing a role makes it extremely difficult to distinguish your specific contribution, efficacy, and impact from your partner's. It is much safer and more effective for assessment purposes to choose distinct roles, even if they are closely related (e.g., a Director and an Assistant Director with clearly delineated responsibilities).

sections.content: The Collaborative Project (Task 2) for HL Theatre is a unique opportunity to function as a genuine theatre company, creating an original piece of theatre from a common stimulus. While collaboration is key, your individual assessment hinges on your specific contribution through a chosen production role. Your process portfolio is not a simple diary; it is a curated, analytical document that must clearly articulate your artistic intentions, practical processes, and the ultimate impact of your work through this specialised lens. This lesson will deconstruct how to excel in your production role and translate that excellence into a top-band portfolio.
Understanding the Spectrum of Production Roles

A production role is your area of specialism within the ensemble. While the director is a common and crucial role, a successful ensemble requires a range of specialists. You are not limited to traditional roles; you can propose a role like 'Dramaturg' or 'Projection Designer' if it is vital to your concept and you can clearly justify it. The key is that the role must have distinct responsibilities and offer sufficient scope for a deep, sustained contribution.

items: **Director:** Shapes the overarching artistic vision, works with performers on interpretation and blocking, and synthesises all production elements. This role is about leadership and holistic artistic control.

**Designer (Set, Costume, Lighting, Sound):** Responsible for a specific aesthetic and functional world. This involves research, conceptualisation (mood boards, sketches), practical realisation (sourcing, building, plotting), and integration with the performance.

**Stage Manager:** The organisational core of the production. Manages schedules, records blocking, creates the prompt book, and is responsible for the technical execution of the show by calling cues. This role is about precision, communication, and logistical control.

**Dramaturg:** A research-focused role. Gathers contextual information, helps structure the narrative, provides feedback to the ensemble, and ensures thematic and theoretical coherence. This role is about intellectual rigour and narrative clarity.

**Props Master/Designer:** Responsible for all properties used in the performance. This involves designing, creating, or sourcing props that are appropriate for the world of the play and functional for the actors.

sections.content: Criterion B: Justifying Your Role Selection

To achieve the top band for Criterion B (7-8 marks), your justification must be 'perceptive, well-reasoned and informed'. This goes far beyond 'I am organised, so I will be the Stage Manager'. A perceptive justification synthesises four key elements: 
1. **The Stimulus:** How does your chosen role specifically allow you to explore the starting stimulus in a unique way? 
2. **Group Vision:** How does your role serve the emerging artistic intentions and needs of the ensemble? 
3. **Personal Skills & Interests:** How do your existing abilities and theatrical passions align with the demands of the role? 
4. **Theatrical Theory/Practice:** How is your choice informed by the work of a specific practitioner or a particular theatrical tradition that you have researched?

sections.question: Write a process portfolio entry (approx. 300 words) justifying your choice of the role of 'Sound Designer' for a collaborative project based on the stimulus of 'the architecture of a beehive'.
sections.solution: My decision to assume the role of Sound Designer is directly informed by the intricate and systemic nature of our stimulus, 'the architecture of a beehive'. The group's intention is to create a non-naturalistic piece exploring themes of collective consciousness and oppressive conformity, inspired by the physical theatre techniques of Frantic Assembly. My role is crucial to this vision, as sound will function not as background atmosphere, but as a primary driver of narrative and emotion. I plan to construct a layered soundscape that embodies the 'hive mind'. This will move beyond literal buzzing to incorporate rhythmic, industrial drones, signifying productivity, which can be distorted into discordant, jarring noise during moments of individual rebellion. This approach is inspired by the work of sound designer Gareth Fry on *Complicité's The Encounter*, where sound creates an immersive, internal world for the audience. My skills in digital audio production will allow me to create this complex, evolving score. As Sound Designer, I can most effectively translate the architectural and systemic qualities of the beehive into an auditory experience that shapes the rhythm of the physical performance and articulates the central tension between the collective and the individual, directly serving the group's conceptual goals.

sections.content: Criterion C: Documenting Your Contribution and Impact

Criterion C assesses your ability to present 'a thorough and perceptive documentation' and 'a sophisticated evaluation of the efficacy and impact' of your contribution. This means your portfolio must be a curated exhibition of your process, not a chronological logbook. For every piece of evidence (a sketch, a cue sheet, a photo), you must provide analytical annotation. Explain the *intention* behind the work, the *process* of its creation, and, most importantly, evaluate its *efficacy* (did it work?) and *impact* (what effect did it have?). Use the language of the theatre. Instead of 'I made the lights red', write 'To heighten the scene's climax, I employed a stark red wash (Lee 189), intending to evoke a sense of danger. In practice, this saturated the stage too heavily, obscuring facial expressions. I therefore refined the cue to a focused red special on the antagonist, which proved more efficacious in directing audience focus while maintaining the intended mood.'

For Criterion C, always use the 'Intention-Process-Impact' model for your portfolio entries. State your artistic **intention**. Show the **process** of trying to achieve it (with visual evidence). Critically evaluate its **impact** and efficacy. This structure ensures you are moving beyond description into the high-level analysis and evaluation that examiners reward.

sections.question: Write a process portfolio entry (approx. 300 words) reflecting on the efficacy of your contribution as 'Costume Designer' during the character development phase.
sections.solution: My initial intention for the protagonist's costume was to reflect their journey from conformity to rebellion using a single, transformative garment. Inspired by the group's Brechtian approach, I designed a grey, uniform-like jumpsuit with concealed, brightly coloured panels that could be revealed. My process involved creating initial sketches, sourcing fabrics, and conducting a fitting with the performer. During a workshop, we discovered that the mechanism for revealing the panels was clumsy and disrupted the flow of the actor's movement, breaking the scene's tension. This demonstrated a lack of efficacy in the initial design; the practical function undermined the symbolic intention. Collaborating with the director and performer, I revised the concept. Instead of a single transformative piece, we developed a series of layering pieces. The character begins in the grey jumpsuit but progressively adds small, personally sourced items of colour—a scarf, a painted patch. The impact of this change was significant. It made the rebellion a more gradual, earned process, better reflecting the narrative arc. This iterative process, though departing from my initial idea, ultimately had a more profound and theatrically effective impact on the performance, demonstrating a successful synthesis of design, character, and collaborative problem-solving.

sections.content: Avoiding Common Pitfalls: Role Bleed and Superficial Engagement

Two major challenges can undermine your success. The first is 'role bleed', where responsibilities are not clearly defined, and everyone has a vague opinion on everything. This leads to a portfolio where it is difficult to isolate your specific contribution. Combat this by creating a 'role agreement' with your group at the start, outlining each person's primary responsibilities and areas of final say. The second pitfall is superficial engagement. This occurs when a student chooses a role but fails to delve into its specific skills and processes. A 'director' who only gives vague notes, or a 'designer' who just brings in items from home without a clear concept, will struggle to produce a portfolio with sufficient depth. True engagement requires research, experimentation, and a commitment to the craft of your chosen role.

items: **Define Boundaries:** At the project's outset, collaboratively write down the key responsibilities for each role to prevent 'role bleed'.
**Focus Your Evidence:** Ensure the evidence in your portfolio is overwhelmingly focused on *your* role. While you must show collaboration, the narrative should be about your specific journey.
**Embrace the Craft:** Go beyond surface-level tasks. Research practitioners in your field, learn the technical vocabulary, and experiment with different techniques.

**Document Challenges:** Don't be afraid to document when things go wrong. A portfolio that shows you identifying a problem with your contribution and finding a solution demonstrates a much higher level of critical reflection.

sections.label: Test Your Understanding of Production Roles
